Quantity of interest:
- Name: Income elasticity of labor supply
- Definition: Percent change in annual hours worked from a 1 percent increase in non-labor income, holding the net-of-tax wage fixed and excluding participation responses.
- Target interpretation: Intensive-margin annual-hours elasticity conditional on employment

runs.reasoningSummary: Standard labor supply theory predicts a negative income effect: higher non-labor income should reduce hours worked, all else equal. citations: Pencavel (1986) on labor supply elasticities
Killingsworth and Heckman (1986) handbook survey

runs.reasoningSummary: Economic theory suggests an income effect: when non-labor income increases, holding real wages constant, individuals have less need to work and typically reduce hours—producing a negative elasticity. Empirical labor supply literature consistently finds income elasticities for prime-age workers are small in magnitude (typically between -0.2 and 0.0), with central estimates near -0.05 to -0.10. The intensive margin (hours conditional on employment) is typically less responsive to income than participation. High uncertainty remains given heterogeneity across subgroups and methodological variation. I centered on a modest negative elasticity with modest spread, assigning non-trivial probability to values near zero and small positive values.
